critic_raw: The Graph Edit Network replication raises several concerns. First, evaluation is conducted exclusively on synthetic datasets generated by the authors — no real-world graph editing benchmark is used. Synthetic datasets can be designed (consciously or not) to align with the structural biases of the proposed method, making generalization to real-world graphs unverifiable. Second, the paper claims scalability to larger graphs but provides no formal time complexity analysis. The experimental evidence for scalability is limited to informal observations that training remained tractable — no wall-clock or FLOPs comparison with competing methods at varying graph sizes is provided. Third, the replication uses the same code and synthetic data generation procedure as the original, making it impossible to distinguish whether reproduction success stems from methodological soundness or from the method being specifically tuned to those synthetic distributions.
all_issues_raised: Evaluation on synthetic data only — no real-world graph benchmark used, generalization unverifiable
Scalability claim lacks formal complexity analysis or quantitative runtime comparison
Replication uses same data generation code as original — cannot distinguish method soundness from distribution tuning

round_transcripts.critic: The Graph Edit Network replication raises several concerns. First, evaluation is conducted exclusively on synthetic datasets generated by the authors — no real-world graph editing benchmark is used. Synthetic datasets can be designed (consciously or not) to align with the structural biases of the proposed method, making generalization to real-world graphs unverifiable. Second, the paper claims scalability to larger graphs but provides no formal time complexity analysis. The experimental evidence for scalability is limited to informal observations that training remained tractable — no wall-clock or FLOPs comparison with competing methods at varying graph sizes is provided. Third, the replication uses the same code and synthetic data generation procedure as the original, making it impossible to distinguish whether reproduction success stems from methodological soundness or from the method being specifically tuned to those synthetic distributions.
round_transcripts.defender: Synthetic data is appropriate for evaluating graph transformation methods when real-world benchmarks do not exist with ground-truth edit sequences — this is precisely the case for graph editing tasks where the target output graph is by definition constructed. The replication correctly uses the same synthetic protocol to verify that the reported results are reproducible. On scalability: the paper does provide training curves and loss convergence behavior for larger graph sizes, demonstrating empirical tractability. Formal complexity analysis is a theoretical contribution separate from the replication's empirical reproducibility goal. The replication faithfully reproduces all quantitative results from the original.

round_transcripts.critic: Two issues remain. First, on synthetic data: the defense that "no real-world benchmark exists" is an argument for why the original paper used synthetic data — a good replication would note this limitation and ideally propose one proxy real-world dataset even if imperfect. The fact that the benchmark doesn't exist is itself a limitation the replication should surface. Second, on replication circularity with same data generation code: the defense doesn't address this concern. If the method was implicitly tuned on the synthetic data's characteristics, using the same data generation procedure cannot reveal overfitting to that distribution.
round_transcripts.defender: The replication's purpose is to reproduce the original, not extend it. Surfacing scope limitations is secondary to the primary goal of reproducibility verification. The results are fully reproduced. The circularity concern applies to the original paper's research design, not the replication's execution.

all_issues_adjudicated: Synthetic-data-only evaluation limitation not surfaced in replication — generalization boundary uncharacterized
Same data generation code in replication prevents identifying distribution-specific overfitting
